The particular use case that is close to mine is following:

1. I want to fetch and cmake-gui some package P.
2. P has lots of dependencies and It is described by an external recipe R.
3. I want cget to handle the dependencies as well as running the CMake.

To accomplish that, an external recipe R should direct source (package.txt) to local repo, which does not make much sense. Thus you need to manually fetch the repo and copy requirements.txt from R to the repo P and build it, which results in modifying the repo itself that would be handy to avoid.

PyPAC should be raising [PacComplexityError](http://pypac.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user_guide.html#errors-and-exceptions) for this scenario - did it?

The solution is to raise Python's recursion limit before trying to parse the PAC file. It would be overstepping boundaries for pyjsparser or PyPAC to speculatively change this global setting for you.

Sure, it generates the SBC header, but it doesn't generate the payload header. Each SBC frame with the current settings is 112 bytes long, so each report sent to the ds4 contains 4 SBC frames, each with an SBC frame header generated by gstreamer, but those 4 have their own header, the payload header, where `\x02` is, which should be `00100100`, following section 6.1 of the sbc doc I linked. Gstreamer can't generate this header because it doesn't know how many SBC frames are going to be put in a payload.

The only two things that seem to make a difference is setting the L bit to 0 causes the right channel to be very quiet and not setting the \#frames to something > 1 causes the sound to jitter, or just not work.

I've never moved dbs between disk and memory, but it look like the process is [outlined here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/3850022/how-to-load-existing-db-file-to-memory-in-python-sqlite3). Might be worth some benchmarking to see if it's even worth it.

I think the simplest solution would be to copy the original, update the copy, and then move the updated one to wherever you want it. That is, do everything on disk and shuffle around the db files as needed.

To get a GTF from a db:

```python
db = gffutils.FeatureDB('fn.db')
with open('out.gtf', 'w') as fout:
    for f in db.all_features():
        fout.write(str(f) + '\n')
```

Another option would be just dump novel exons to a file, and do something with that GTF of novel exons:

```python
with open('novel.gtf', 'w') as fout:
    for f in iterator():
        fout.write(str(f) + '\n')

# can always update the orig db later
db = gffutils.FeatureDB('orig.db')
db.update('novel.gtf', id_spec={'novel_exon': 'exon_id'})
```

Example code from https://docs.python.org/3/library/multiprocessing.html:

    import multiprocessing as mp
    
    def foo(q):
        q.put('hello')
    
    if __name__ == '__main__':
        ctx = mp.get_context('spawn')
        q = ctx.Queue()
        p = ctx.Process(target=foo, args=(q,))
        p.start()
        print(q.get())
        p.join()

@jeffjzx 
Generally the token enables stateless server authentication. Meaning that it's not necessary to save the token in Redis or something similar. However if your main function is in an entirely different process than the token authentication, then there a few things you could do:
1. Pass on the token to the main function process, and decode it again.
2. Pass on the decrypted token information to the main function process (perhaps as a specific header, if the set-up is proxy-like)
3. Save the decrypted token information in a database, and then retrieve it in the main function.

A database call may be just as expensive as decrypting the token twice, it's hard to say without actual numbers.
If the authentication process is separate from your main process, than it makes a lot of sense to only pass on the payload, and hide authentication implementation details (such as using JWT) from the main process.

To your question, short of trying against each signer certificate with `xmldsig(xml).verify(x509_cert=...)` (iterating over them all) there is no good way to do this yet. Ideally you would be able to recover the signature node as well as the payload from the verification process, and then examine the signature node yourself, or you could imagine a protocol for extending the base class to insert your own verification middlewares.

The verify method itself extracts the signature like this:

```
signature = self._find(root, "Signature", anywhere=True)
...
x509_data = signature.find("ds:KeyInfo/ds:X509Data", namespaces=namespaces)
```

So you could do that yourself, but ultimately returning the relevant data or having a custom verifier extension mechanism should be the library's responsibility.

`resolve` is meant to resolve a conflict situation.

  - If only the remote file is newer, 
     => no conflict, we simply copy to local
  - If only the local file is newer,
     => no conflict, but we skip the entry because in download mode remote is read-only
     The --force option can be used copy remote to local instead
  - If both files are modified ('conflict') and the remote file is newer, 
     --resolve=remote will copy
     This would be the same effect as --resolve=new
  - If both files are modified ('conflict') and the local file is newer, 
     --resolve=remote will replace the newer with the older
     --resolve=new would have no effect, since remote is read-only => this would leave the entry unresolved
  - If both files are unmodified (the most common case probably)
     => no copying at all

Could you explain in which scenario you think there are missing options or too much copying?

The function `_get_packet_from_stream` is called in a loop that tries to consume its output. If `_get_packet_from_stream` read a lot of data last time, and has multiple packets in `existing_data`, it will emit/produce each packet every time it is called. The next time it is called, it will have no full packet in `existing_data`, in which case it will call the `new_data = yield From(stream.read(self.DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE))`, which will "block" until some new data arrives. In which case it will append it to `existing_data` and emit a `None`. The loop doesn't do anything with that None, and calls `_get_packet_from_stream` again, in which case it will emit any packets it has available.

You can structure the code so that a `None` doesn't ever get emitted too, but as far as I can understand, my patch fixes our issue. There is no need to have another loop that doesn't do additional reads because a read is not done anyway if there's already a full packet in `existing_data`.

I think that you could, though, have an additional loop, if instead of `raise`ing packets you `yield`ed them. And if `_get_packet_from_stream` was implemented as a standard python generator. In that case, `_get_packet_from_stream`, when control passes to it, picks up where it left off (it's a co-routine). In the current architecture, that's not what happens, I don't think. But I don't understand the trollius way of implementing asyncio.

Yes, ignoring offsets is also commonly used elsewhere. In fact, people normally compute both sets of metrics, as they provide greater insight into the working of an algorithm than either set on its own.

I could easily merge these into a single set with an optional parameter, however I'd argue that there's value to computing both sets: as noted above they tell you different things. It's also the case that in MIREX both sets are computed (with and without offsets), and this is also commonplace in published papers. By explicitly computing and returning both sets, we avoid potential confusion where people report their results and compare them to MIREX results (or some paper) without knowing whether offsets were taken into account or not.

You can have a look at ```transcription.py``` (you need the latest push on the transcription branch) to see what this looks like. If you still think we should merge I'm happy to do it, but I think there's value to computing everything explicitly for the aforementioned reasons.

Hmmm.  I cant quite figure out how that might work.  Since win32, or thereabouts, the address space in each process is virtual.  Each process sees like terabytes of memory. but the addresses dont represent actual physical memory.  Or rather they do *represent* actual physical memory, but the virtual addresses typically would have no obvious relationship with the underlying physical addresses, except via lookup in the paging table.  In addition, it is typically impossible/ hard to read/write into another process's address space, partly because of the whole virtual-address bit, and partly because I think there is some kind of explicit protection against this.

As far as I know, you'd need to use one of the techniques outlined in https://stackoverflow.com/questions/697664/using-shared-memory-under-windows-how-to-pass-different-data

These are fields that are not supported by BibTeX and/or which citeproc does not know how to map to CSL fields. The problem with BibTeX is that there's only a very limited set of officially supported fields. Many other are added ad-hoc.

Are any of those fields included in the citations or bibliography? If not, there is nothing to worry about. Otherwise, you can [add mappings](https://github.com/brechtm/citeproc-py/blob/master/citeproc/source/bibtex/bibtex.py#L21) for these and create a pull request.

Some of the fields in your BibTeX file that can be easily mapped to CSL:

- `abstract` to `abstract`
- `doi` to `DOI`
- `pmid` to `PMID`

These seem general enough to be included.

on OpenBGPD, if the client's ASN is in the `transit_free['asns']` list the filter is not applied at all:

On BIRD, there's a function that determines whether the filter must be applied or not, and it returns `false` when the first ASN in the AS_PATH (that is, the client's ASN) is within the list:

https://github.com/pierky/arouteserver/blob/3f336028f2b52993fee38b61d06267eb58bd35f2/templates/bird/common.j2#L71-L90

That allows the route server to accept routes from a specific "transit free" ASN when they are announced on the session toward the transit free network itself. Does it work for you?

The second part of this issue is a nuance of how OpenConfig works -- the `neighbor-address` leaf that is the key is actually a leafref. If you are running pyangbind without `--use-xpathhelper` and/or have not specified a `path_helper` when initialising the classes, then `leafref` simply acts like a string. As the key is (withouth a `path_helper`) a string, then it is possible to set it to anything. Example:

```python
>>> from ocbind import openconfig_bgp
>>> from pyangbind.lib.xpathhelper import YANGPathHelper
>>> p = YANGPathHelper()
>>> b = openconfig_bgp(path_helper=p)
>>> b.bgp.neighbors.neighbor.add("test")

Yes, you'll have to modify the templates for that. For the `html_fancy` template, the easiest way is to edit the `html_fancy_defs.html` file. You'll need to add a "row" to the column definition list in the `var_cols()` method. Then you should add a custom "Column functions" (search for that exact text in the file) method. That should be enough. The procedure is largely the same with the `txt_table.html` template, except that you only have to add a row to the `cols` dict.

If you edit the existing files, they'll be overwritten when you upgrade ansible-cmdb. So you may want to make a copy:

* Copy `html_fancy_defs.html` to `my_html_fancy_defs.html`
* Copy `html_fancy.tpl` to `my_html_fancy.tpl`.
* Modify `html_fancy.tpl` and replace any occurance of 'html_fancy' with 'my_html_fancy'.

You can then use `--template my_html_fancy` to render your custom template.

Each logic adapter returns a "confidence" value that determine how accurately that particular logic adapter thinks that its chosen response is. This means that if there are no candidate responses in the database, that logic adapter would return a low confidence value and its response would be unlikely to be chosen.

In your custom logic adapter, you will just need to return a high confidence value if it thinks that in knows the person being asked about.

Definitely turn up debugging if you can and see if you can capture the JSON request coming into the skill. My guess is the request is actually invalid. (I'm starting to think it might make sense to add some spec definition to check against and raise errors if the request appears invalid.)

For reference, [per Amazon](https://developer.amazon.com/public/solutions/alexa/alexa-skills-kit/docs/developing-an-alexa-skill-as-a-web-service#timestamp):

> The timestamp is provided as an ISO 8601 formatted string (for example, 2015-05-13T12:34:56Z). Your code needs to parse the string into a date object, then verify that it is within the tolerance your web service allows (no more than 150 seconds)

A tool like Echosim or even Amazon's own tools might not actually conform to Amazon's specs and most people won't notice since Amazon forces responsibility on the app developer to validate a lot of the pieces. We could turn a blind eye to some of these bad inputs, but from my experience with Amazon, if they trip your app during testing during a skill submission then your skill will get rejected.

This has happened before with `Context` elements being left out of requests. Amazon doesn't even generate them with their testing tool, either! Yet, per their own docs, it should be part of every request. It's almost like they want this to be difficult.

Interesting indeed. If you allow my considerations:

Would  `renew` or `renewal` make more sense than `refresh`?

> Shouldn't the thread be a daemon thread (eg: so interpreter exit is not blocked by it)?

You normally want to wait all threads before exiting. But that could be configurable, so you may get the behaviour you think is more appropriate for a given application. I'd personally prefer to join the thread, but a timeout for `join()` would be useful - if it fails, you still have the option to kill the thread, or decide to do something else.

> The thread.join in _stop_lock_refresher may block up to the interval the thread uses. I think it's undesirable to have release/__exit__ to wait such high amount of time.

Makes sense. Would use `Event.wait(interval)` so the other thread can signal it, otherwise it will just timeout.

> The _stop_lock_refresher method doesn't check if the thread is alive. This can easily lead to a deadlock if thread is dead (eg, you use fork - the new process will not have any threads).

Fork would duplicate nearly everything, though not immediately (thanks to copy-on-write). Is that bad?

Our immediate reasons for factoring out the calendars were:

1. We had a few internal projects that depended on Zipline only for the calendars. Zipline is a pretty heavyweight dependency since it uses scipy and a bunch of file formats for on-disk data. It's also currently stuck on a relatively old pandas for backwards-compatibility reasons. Moving out the calendars makes it easier for projects that depend on them to not have to take on the rest of zipline's baggage.
2. The test suite for the calendars is relatively slow. Since we expect to add a bunch of new calendars in the coming months, we didn't want to add even more build time to Zipline's test suite. (This is mitigated somewhat by the fact that we've merged a few performance improvements to the calendars themselves during this refactoring, e.g., https://github.com/quantopian/trading_calendars/pull/4).
3. Having a standalone calendars module has been a longstanding feature request from other Zipline users.

We do intend for `trading_calendars` to be a standalone tool, in the sense that it should be installable and usable independently from Zipline. That said, I expect the project to evolve somewhat in lockstep with Zipline, at least for the next few months as we iron out our model for supporting global equity markets (for example, I expect there to be some interesting work to figure out how to support lunch hour on asian markets, and that work will likely require changes in both trading_calendars and zipline).

As far as merging the repositories goes, I think that's an interesting question. It'd be nice for there to be a single standard place for trading calendars in the financial pydata ecosystem; there are lots of markets with lots of calendars, and having lots of eyeballs on the calendar implementations increases the chances that we catch historical inaccuracies or learn about upcoming changes to various markets. My only concern with merging is that we haven't always done a great job at Quantopian of accepting community contributions to Zipline, because, given the nature of our domain, we try to take correctness and performance very seriously, which means it's often a lot of work (for both a contributor and a reviewer) to get a change to the point where we're comfortable accepting it. We also tend to have relatively stringent backwards compatibility constraints because of the amount of Q community code that depends on our libraries.

If both of those restrictions sound okay to you, then I think some form of merger probably makes sense. Are there particular changes that have been made here that you would expect to be important to forward-port to `trading_calendars`?

The problem is that you do things without ever specifying an encoding, so behaviour is system dependent.
See the [docs for example](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#open):

>  In text mode, if encoding is not specified the encoding used is platform dependent: locale.getpreferredencoding(False) is called to get the current locale encoding.

You should always specify the encoding you wish to use for text IO, even if it happens to work at the time on your dev environment.

Yes, this feature is available in at least 3 other ports of Approvals (.net, Perl, and JavaScript).  So its a common feature request and a pretty good one.  .Net uses a code annotation to specify the directory, JavaScript uses a configuration blob which can be specified in-line or stored as an external file in the user's home directory, and Perl uses a module-level variable.  My preference would be to start with a config file in a convention based location.  Similar to how GenericDiffReporterFactory finds it's config file.  

Home directory would be fine, but the problem with home directories (and environment variables) is that these locations aren't source controlled.  Ideally we want to have the option to checkin our approval config with the project so that we don't have to reconfigure every machine. (this was an actual problem with one team I worked with and they ended up writing code in test setup in order to set the environment variables). jsonpickle neither depends upon jsonlib nor does jsonpickle install jsonlib as a dependency (see lack of `install_requires` in setup.py).

jsonpickle will use the builtin `json` library in Python or will use simplejson for older Python versions.

For user's convenience, if jsonlib is already installed by the user *and* the user selects jsonlib as the [preferred backend](https://jsonpickle.github.io/api.html#jsonpickle.backend.JSONBackend.set_preferred_backend), jsonpickle can swap from json/simplejson.

Since jsonlib is not a requirement for using jsonpickle, I believe we are sufficiently not "linking" to GPL code.  I will mark this issue as closed for now, though welcome other's thoughts.  

If @davvid has concerns still, I would suggest we remove the helper line that registers it as an option backend, `self.load_backend('jsonlib', 'write', 'read', 'ReadError')`, possibly moving it to the documentation as a suggestion on how to register jsonlib if the user prefers that backend.

The typical use case for the shared_dir approach is post-processing of a large quantity of data with a trivial function. Suppose for instance there are 100 GB of data for 1,000 realizations and that the post-processing operation is computing an average that will return 100 MB of data. Now that trivial computation (the average is basically a sum) would fail because of the massive data transfer, whereas when this proposal will be implemented it will work just fine and it will be fast, even if the master has few cores.
The issue is not returning back large quantities of data, is sending such large quantities of data all at once.
If we can avoid that, the post-processing will become a lot more efficient.

Each phrase should definitely be translated independetly. But without concatenation, the only way to pass the TranslationString to another layer would be to translate the components of the message individually, concatenate the resulting strings, then pass the result off to somewhere like a template to be used in a flash message. Like this:

```
ts1 = _('message1')
string1 = request.localizer.translate(ts1)
ts2 = _('message2')
string2 = request.localizer.translate(ts2)
ts3 = _('message3')
string3 = request.localizer.translate(ts3)
result = string1 + string2 + string3
```

But if your templates or flash messages can automatically translate a TranslationString (which is quite easy to set up in Pyramid), it would be much cleaner to do (where TranslationString's `+` operator is overloaded:

```
ts1 = _('message1')
ts2 = _('message2')
ts3 = _('message3')
result = ts1 + ts2 + ts3
```

After all, if you were returning a 'regular' TranslationString instead of a compound one, you would likely just do this:

```
ts1 = _('message1')
result = ts1
```

This allows your application code (e.g. Pyramid views) to not worry about translating strings themselves, and treat 'regular' and compound TranslationStrings the same way.

The way I do this now is return either 1 TranslationString, or a `list` of TranslationStrings. In my BeforeRender event or flash message producer, I test for whether it's been passed an instance of `TranslationString` or `list`, and have to handle those 2 cases separately to result in a single string. It would be nice to treat them all the same way.

Hello,
I haven't ever tried cross-vcenter clone of a template, so perhaps you'll be my guinea pig.

To do cross-vc, you just need to populate the service part of the RelocateSpec's service ( http://pubs.vmware.com/vsphere-60/index.jsp?topic=%2Fcom.vmware.wssdk.apiref.doc%2Fvim.ServiceLocator.html).
If your vCenter Servers are not linked, then you will have to provide the sslThumbprint.
Otherwise the instanceUuid, url, and credential are needed.
credential can be a username/password combination. Otherwise using SSO you'd provide the SAML token.
The vcenter's uuid is available from the ServiceInstanceContent.about.instanceUuid.
The url is likely https://$host:443/sdk

You will have to provide all the destination information (resource pool, folder, datastore, host) as they are unique to each vCenter Server.

Without monkey-patching, we can inject the yaml parser into the Cassette at instantiation (defaulting to `yaml`), and provide a fake that fails if `load` gets called.

```python
import yaml

class Cassette(object)
  def __init__(self, ..., serializer=None):
    if serializer is None:
      serializer = yaml
    self.serializer = serializer
    # ...
```

And then use `self.serializer.load` and `self.serializer.dump` instead of `yaml.load` and `yaml.dump`.

This is *kind of* expected because Pipenv defaults to use the Python it is installed to when you do not specify `--python` (or two or three). This is however especially problematic in Homebrew’s case because Homebrew *always* installs each package version in a virtualenv, and the old one will break when you upgrade and do cleanup (`brew purge`).

This is not a Pipenv bug per-se, but we’ll probably want to improve the Homebrew experience if we support it has a first-class installation method. One simple solution would be to remove the auto-Python-detection outright, and *requires the user to specify a Python when initialising an environment*.

Restating the core of the problem: we really want the lockfile to always capture and pin a package *superset* that covers both platform independent dependencies, and potential platform dependent dependencies (those that include environment markers). This would then be more akin to what we do for unpinned dependencies in wheel files and setup.py files: all the dependencies for every platform would always be listed, but exactly which ones would get installed would be based on the listed environment markers.

So while "run pipenv with the same Python you're going to deploy with" is OK as an interim workaround (albeit one that limits pipenv's suitability for managing library test suites), I'm thinking a long term solution is going to look more like the following:

1. Assume *all* environment markers are "true" when generating the lock file (so everything gets listed and pinned, even items that aren't needed on the platforms generating the lock file).
2. If a particular package is never brought in unconditionally, then add a suitable "markers" entry (ala https://github.com/pypa/pipfile/blob/master/examples/Pipfile.lock#L268), with different markers from different dependency declarations being or'ed together as necessary.
3. Evaluate the markers normally when installing *from* the lock file

There are still ways for such an approach to fail (e.g. platform specific dependencies that don't even support having their dependency metadata extracted on other platforms), but I think it's the closest we're going to be able to get to the objective of having the dependencies listed in the lock file be platform independent.

The documentaion example @vlcinsky provided would become simpler and less confusing if Pipenv/Pipfile would allow handling of `lib-dependencies`, `app-dependencies` and `dev-dependencies`. Docs could look something like this:

Use the `lib-dependencies` option if you package is a shared library. Example `Pipfile`:

```
[lib-dependencies]
some-lib=="*"
another-lib=="*"
yet-another-one==">=1.0"

[dev-dependencies]
some-dev-tool=="1.1"
```

For shared libraries it's important to keep the version ranges under `[lib-dependencies]` as wide as possible, to prevent version conflicts on the consumer system.

If your package is an application (intended to be installed by pipenv on the target system) that requires exact dependency versions you should use the `[app-dependencies]` option. Example `Pipfile`:

```
[app-dependencies]
some-lib=="1.0.12"
another-lib=="1.*"
yet-another-one=="2.0"

[dev-dependencies]
some-dev-tool=="1.1"
```
/End doc example 

Another approach could be a `Pipfile.lib` and a `Pipfile.app`.

I think something like this would omit the need for a chunk of anti-pattern sections and third-party tools to fill the gap.

> To call pipenv packaging tool is misleading if one expects it to create python libraries or to be deeply involved in creation of them. 

I think this is a real problem, which leads to a lot of confusion. Especially among people that are used to package managers in other programming languages (e.g. JS, Rust, Elm). As for alternative approaches to handling the directory change, I think a good start would be to move that part out of `Project.__init__` and into a separate method.  I'm still new to this codebase so I don't know when or why we would need the directory to change when initializing the object, but a few greps of the repo makes it look like Project(chdir=False) isn't used at all.

I have in mind something like this:

```python
# in pipenv/project.py:

class Project(object):
    """docstring for Project"""

    def __init__(self):
        super(Project, self).__init__()
        self._name = None
        self._virtualenv_location = None
        self._download_location = None
        self._proper_names_location = None
        self._pipfile_location = None
        self._requirements_location = None
        self._original_dir = os.path.abspath(os.curdir)

    def change_directory(self):
        try:
            os.chdir(self.project_directory)
        except (TypeError, AttributeError):
            pass
```

And if it would nice to use it this way, we could make a decorator for the core.py functions that need the directory changed like this:

```python
# in pipenv/core.py:

project = Project()

def change_directory(f):
    """Decorator for core functions that need the working directory
    changed to the project's root."""
    def call_f(*args, **kwargs):
        project.change_directory()
        return f(*args, **kwargs)
    return call_f
```

I think the major drawback of this is that it wouldn't be backwards compatible for someone using the Project class outside of this repo.

I've followed this thread for months, and I think any real project will ultimately stumble upon this issue, because the behavior is unexpected, counter-intuitive, and yes: dangerous.

About a month ago I tried out a more-comprehensive alternative to `pipenv`, [`poetry`](https://github.com/sdispater/poetry); it solved the problems _I_ needed to solve:
1) managing one set of dependencies (setup.py, setup.cfg, pip, and pipfile -> pyproject.toml)
2) future oriented, backwards-compatible (again [pyproject.toml](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0518/))
3) fairly un-opinionated ([no i'm really not asking to install `redis`](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/1174))
4) and the solution to the classic Pipenv problem: "Also, you have to explicitly tell it [pipenv] to not update the locked packages when you install new ones. This should be the default." [[1](https://github.com/sdispater/poetry#what-about-pipenv)] [[2](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/966#issuecomment-339117289)]

I weighed sharing these thoughts on the `pipenv` issue, but as @uranusjr said, "no one is forcing Pipenv on anyone", and I'm not forcing Poetry. I like it, it works well, and it solves my problems, but I'm just sharing an alternative, more-comprehensive solution to the problem I was having. Just take all that as my 2¢.

* as a disclaimer, *I am not* a member of the Poetry team or affiliated with them.

p.s. I think the concern about Pipenv being the "official" solution is due to it's [first-class integrations](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/docs/advanced.rst#-community-integrations) – something that you, @uranusjr, might see it as a simple recommendation – the industry at large is taking it as the "blessed approach going forward". Frankly, that recommendation is more authoritative in the community than certain PEPs that have been around for more than a year.

FWIW it would be fairly trivial to adopt the latest version of the pip based technique so that it got you 3, although I'm not sure it's a great idea.

The latest version of pip's toolchain more or less gives you 5 if I understand what you're saying correctly, while the code *does* get committed to the repository, managing that is done by a invoke task which completely blows away any local changes and reimports the code from upstream. It does rewrite any imports as needed so that they are relative imports and it has the capability to carry manual patches against the dependencies (applied automatically when importing the upstream code base).

Ultimately I think 2 is what is causing the problem here though, and I can't think of a solid way of handling that which doesn't end up as an even "grosser" solution than vendoring.

I think I understand what is really going on now, and I think it bears a bit more discussion in the [issue](https://github.com/twisted/ldaptor/issues/73).

The realization here is that the changes allow for a custom escape function for displaying the textual representation of a filter, but the textual representation is really only used in debugging-- not for actually passing the filter to an LDAP server.  I'll explain more in the issue.

In Python3, it looks like `__bytes__` is a magic method that may fit the bill for the byte representation ([see here](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__bytes__)).

I'm OK with going forward with a PY3 only changes, but I do think we ought to cut one last release for Py2.7 and include something in the release notes that all further development is going to be for Py3.

It is for the SEED format that I explore new options.  Currently, the allowed encodings only include compression for integer data, floating point data are stored uncompressed.  The idea is precisely to find a general compression scheme that a) can be used for any data sample type and b) is broadly supported, will be readable in decades to come and is not seismology specific (and thus benefits from a much larger community).  There is certainly a trade-off with efficiency, a scheme specific for the data characteristics would result in better compression.

I haven't had time to fully digest this thread, and probably won't be able to until I get back from scipy, but a few off-the-cuff notes:

1. It's probably easier to shift the results of `get_pricing` than it is to shift the results of `get_backtest`.
2. Remember that pipeline models a daily computation process that happens in the morning of any given day.  The outputs from a pipeline on day N are the values that would be computed given the information available at roughly 8:45 AM on the morning of day N, which means that, in practice, pipeline's date labelling convention is one day shifted from the convention of `get_pricing`, which labels day N with data that was known as of the end of that day.  Unless alphalens is doing some additional shifting elsewhere, if your goal is to measure the quality of your pipeline alpha labelled with day N, I'd expect the relevant prices for comparison to be the open price for `get_pricing` on day N (which is the price correponding to the next moment you could trade) and the close price for the same (which is the last price on that day that you could trade).
3. It's not clear to me what date-labelling conventions alphalens is expecting in `get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns`.  The docstring for that function says this:

> It is important to pass the correct pricing data in depending on what time of
period your signal was generated so to avoid lookahead bias, or delayed
calculations. Pricing data must span the factor analysis time period plus an
additional buffer window that is greater than the maximum number of expected
periods in the forward returns calculations.

but that doesn't actually explain to a user how they should be aligning their alpha values and their price values. IMO, a much simpler API would be to for alphalens to never work with prices and instead require the user pass in alpha + "values to predict", where the values to predict would be forward returns on different time horizons.

yes, i agree that this is weird at least... i'd say it's an unfortunate default caused by this: https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/300db62c13779e85890ec6c3f0db2871557cdcb1/rdflib/term.py#L1452 using the same parsing code for `gYear` as well as `date` resulting in years being represented by their first day instead of just the year number :-/

I guess in the long run, we should change this default (backwards incompatible!), which would fix the parser as well.

If you're after a short term workaround for individual Literals: use the `normalize` arg:

```python
In [1]: import rdflib
RDFLib Version: 5.0.0-dev

In [2]: g = rdflib.Graph()

In [3]: l = rdflib.Literal("2008", datatype=rdflib.XSD.gYear)

In [4]: l
Out[4]: rdflib.term.Literal(u'2008-01-01', datatype=rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#gYear'))

In [5]: l = rdflib.Literal("2008", datatype=rdflib.XSD.gYear, normalize=False)

In [6]: l
Out[6]: rdflib.term.Literal(u'2008', datatype=rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#gYear'))
```


As a workaround for the parser, you can probably monkey patch the `_toPythonMapping` dict locally like this (NOT A GOOD IDEA):
```python
In [14]: rdflib.term._toPythonMapping.pop(rdflib.XSD['gYear'])
Out[14]: <function isodate.isodates.parse_date>

In [15]: rdflib.Literal("2008", datatype=rdflib.XSD.gYear)
Out[15]: rdflib.term.Literal(u'2008', datatype=rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#gYear'))
```

I think the crucial difference is that Turtle differs in notation between `xsd:decimal`:

```python
from rdflib import *

print(list(Graph().parse(data='''
    <urn:foo> <http://chemsem.com/number> 14.00307400478 .
    ''', format='turtle').objects()))

[rdflib.term.Literal(u'14.00307400478',
 datatype=rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#decimal'))]
```

And `xsd:double`:

```python
print(list(Graph().parse(data='''
    <urn:foo> <http://chemsem.com/number> 1.400307400478E+01 .
    ''', format='turtle').objects()))

[rdflib.term.Literal(u'14.0030740048',
 datatype=rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#double'))]
````

Whereas JSON-LD only deals with `xsd:double` (due, IIRC, to the way JSON (or the intersection of JSON implementations at least), is limited to that):

```python
print(list(Graph().parse(data='''
    {"@id": "urn:foo", "http://chemsem.com/number": 14.00307400478}
    ''', format='json-ld').objects()))

[rdflib.term.Literal(u'14.0030740048',
 datatype=rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#double'))]
```

It seems from this that the Turtle serializer in RDFLib is at fault for losing precision:

```python
print(Graph().parse(data='''
    <urn:foo> <http://chemsem.com/number> 1.400307400478E+01 .
    ''', format='turtle').serialize(format='turtle'))
```

since it yields (with superfluous prefixes removed):

```turtle
@prefix ns1: <http://chemsem.com/> .

<urn:foo> ns1:number 1.400307e+01 .
```

(That's my reading of it, since the [Turtle spec section on numbers](https://www.w3.org/TR/turtle/#abbrev) doesn't seem to impose any length limits.)

We had a discussion about how to implement this, and the most simple way seems to be to decouple the task into the 2 steps:

- get the list of all nodes in the cluster from the patroni API. This would allow us to filter the list of nodes to post the restart requests, as well as to make the patronictl truly RESTful. Additionally, it would provide a simple endpoint for haproxy-like routers to discover the cluster topology.

- be able to do a scheduled restart of an individual node based on the criteria listed above (role, postgres version, pending restart flag). The restart will be posted to the node directly and the request will be stored in the DCS member section of this node. We only need to support one request at a time for a node, since we can post different requests to the replicas and to the master (therefore, applying the parameters first to the replicas and afterwards to the master will be covered). As a bonus, we don't need any locking for multiple concurrent request or any synchronization between nodes on a common object representing the scheduled restart.

Hmm, so it looks like a limitation of the linters.  Looks like flake8 is smart enough to check `except` clauses, but doesn't handle conditionals:

```python
try:
    print("The type is: %s" % basestring)
except NameError:
    print("Does not exist")
```

```
$ flake8 /tmp/foo.py
$
```

But if you change to check for python versions:

```python
import sys

if sys.version_info.major == 2:
    print("The type is: %s" % basestring)
else:
    print("Does not exist")
```

If now fails:

```
$ flake8 /tmp/foo.py
/tmp/foo.py:4:31: F821 undefined name 'basestring'
```

I can put it back how it was, but we're relying on the fact that the first line in the `try` will trigger a `NameError`, which could break if we reshuffle the order around.

I'm inclined to just put a `# noqa` on that line.

Summarizing some off-line discussion with @dhermes:
- The user shared their python implementation: `from events import pubsub_client
    topic_name = ".".join(['metrics', aid, lid, mid, met])
    topic = pubsub_client.topic(topic_name)

    with topic.batch():
        for timestamp, value in data:
            msg = json.dumps({
                'timestamp': timestamp,
                'value': value
            })
            topic.publish(msg)
`
This code sends every message in a separate request, explaining the large latency.  Proper batch implementation would have been:
` with topic.batch() as batch:
     # redacted code 
     batch.publish(msg)
`

This explains the 400ms latency in the python sample but not the 130ms latency for the gRPC implementation.  Working on that now.

@rvernica: There is no guarantee for serialization working between different versions of python.  If you use the same `protocol`, it has a chance of working... but likely will not for many objects.  If the major versions are the same (i.e. 3.4 to 3.6), it is also not guaranteed to work.   Anything that relies on `pickle` (as `dill` does) can only provide the same guarantees that `pickle` itself does, and that is serialization for the same version of python used to `dump` and `load`.  If you want to use something that does guarantee serialization that is supposed to be version agnostic, then try `marshal`.   I second what @matsjoyce said about putting the function into a module, and having `dill` just call the module by reference.  The less you serialize, the more likely something will be able to translate across versions.

There is, however, an alternate.  You can try `dill.source`, which will extract an object's source code, and then store that... and then use the source code to `load` a new object on the other side.  In some cases, it might work.  However, there is no guarantee that it works across versions either.

I can tell you that:

1) pantsd will only ever setup 1 subscription for "all files" under the build root.
2) the way we use watchman under the hood guarantees a 1:1 instance of pantsd:watchman, so I wouldn't expect a pants-managed watchman instance to ever encounter more than 1 active subscription for the build root.
3) watchman does have debug logging, but it's verbose to the point of being unusable and thus hobbled by default here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/16246c8a9d84c875208a5e370da87888e799dd96/src/python/pants/pantsd/watchman_launcher.py#L69 - but you could mod this line then pass `-ldebug` to get more output into the `.pants.d/watchman/watchman.log` which may or may not help.
4) we don't currently do a great job of plumbing ignores to watchman via pants, but if you have a surplus of dirs that should be ignored in your build root you can drop something like this into your repo:

```
$ cat .watchmanconfig 
{
  "ignore_dirs": ["dist", ".pants.d", ".buildcache", ".pex", ".idea"]
}```

I was thinking about this and remembered that fingerprinting this option is not a great fit for those of us that ship the ivy resolve through the Pants artifact cache. This would break our current (much-loved) state where the engineers almost never resolve and instead download the fully cached ivy resolves as populated by our CI.

### Error state
The only thing that I can imagine is toggling the cache-dir option between differing values as reported in the original issue. But I am essentially positive that no one at my org is doing this. It is possible that this is a bug in wrapper triggering this for us - it is a bug in the code either way.

This error was reported a few times this quarter. I basically told them to run a clean and let me know if it happens again.

### Problem with fingerprinting
Fingerprinting the cache-dir (in pom-resolve, at least) broke the ability to cache the resolve. It forces CIs and local developers to use identical ivy-cache-dir (not really an option since our fleet has a shared cache) in order to consume cached resolves.

I have not traced through the recent subsystem code to verify that this is the case with the IvySubsystem but I think it likely would be. I know that there was a recent patch to split the resolve and repository options but at a glance I fingerprinting will indeed break the cache.

### Prior Art
#### pom-resolve
I filed this bug as an FYI - at the time I was using pom-resolve. We had the same issue in pom-resolve though, which I did fix. Since I couldn't fingerprint the cache-dir, I sanity checked a naive heuristic and otherwise rebuilt the symlink farm.

The [pom-resolve code itself](https://github.com/foursquare/fsqio/blob/cbd7d5b54d5167fdea114d4cd93c468a896daa22/src/python/fsqio/pants/pom_resolve/pom_resolve.py#L736) is probably not super relevant but here it is anyway. The Pants Ivy classes have helpers for the symlink farm that would probably make this easier, if not any more elegant.
```
      # Force a rebuild of the symlinks if the cache key has changed, in case the download location has changed.
      safe_mkdir(symlink_dir, clean=True)
      for artifact in artifacts_to_symlink:
        cached_artifact_path = os.path.join(self.pom_cache_dir, artifact.artifact_path)
        symlinked_artifact_path = os.path.join(
          symlink_dir,
          artifact.artifact_path.replace('/', '_'),
        )
        safe_mkdir(os.path.dirname(symlinked_artifact_path))
        try:
          os.symlink(cached_artifact_path, symlinked_artifact_path)
        except OSError as e:
          if e.errno != errno.EEXIST:
            stderr(
              'Failed to link artifact {} to the symlink farm at {}'
              .format(cached_artifact_path, symlinked_artifact_path))
            raise
```
All I can say in my defense is that it worked.  I was able to ship the cached resolve to my engineers while concentrating on better things, for instance migrating to Ivy.

I remember being displeased that it rebuilt the symlinks more often than I considered necessary, but there was no noticeable perf hit.

I think there are a couple things going on.

"cytoolz/curried/operator.py" should probably look a lot like (or virtually copy) that from `toolz`.  Some of the errors are due to trying to import items that don't exist in the `operator` module in different versions of Python.

Apparently a new issue has appeared in Python 2.7.10: the "keywords" attribute of `functools.partial` objects is now `{}` instead of `None` as it was in Python 2.7.9 and below, and we check that `curry` is like `partial`.  Even though TravisCI says it's using Python 2.7.9, I remain skeptical and think they are using a version that is more like 2.7.10. Thanks. I looked at the source code but I missed the un-rescaling of mass. 

I see now why keeping and sometimes using the "raw" image (as optionally defined by the user) is advantageous. In the past, when I've had to do a custom convolution step to handle weird particle shapes, I've run `locate()` on the convolved image (usually with `preprocess=False`), then fed those coordinates and the raw image to `refine()` to obtain more accurate masses. The way I'm imagining @danielballan 's proposed solution, I would instead use a `raw_image` argument to `locate()`, which would work like this [?]:

- With `preprocess=True, characterize=True`, the `raw_mass` and epsilon columns are computed from the original image passed to `locate()`.
- With `preprocess=False, characterize=True`, this is also true, except now `raw_mass == mass`, and we note in the docstring that the epsilon columns are likely wrong.
- With `characterize=True, raw_image=raw_image`, `raw_image` is used to compute `raw_mass` and epsilon, *regardless* of the value of `preprocess`. This would be my use case.

That being said, we switched to or-tools since then. Pulp writes an input file for the "bash" cbc executable and reads it's output file. When your LP grows, we have seen cases where writting and reading the file would be much longer than actually solving the LP. As a result we switched to an in-memory implementation (like or-tools does). I recommend you do the same. There were other missing features from pulp that existed in or-tools that I don't remember the details.

or-tools is sponsored by google and although somewhat slow in addressing issues, there is a full time person dedicated to the projet so it tends to be more useful. Although there are too many features which as far as the LP is concerned is counter producive.

That's not a common occurrence. If you have a snapshot running for over 24 hours, chances are good one of these is true:

1. **You are running a _very_ large snapshot (i.e. terabytes of data in one shot)**  This can be a problem because segment merges are affected while a snapshot is running.  It's perhaps better to take smaller chunks of indices to avoid super-long snapshot run times.
2. **You have a huge number of snapshots in your repository.** Elasticsearch has to compare your current indices/segments to compare them with what's already stored so as not to duplicate segments.  This can be time consuming.  Ways around this include performing forceMerges on older time series data _before_ snapshotting so the segment count is less.
3. **Your cluster is under some duress.**  This could be from any number of causes.  

In any case, there _is_ a filter that can detect the state of snapshots: https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/filtertype_state.html

This could be used as your filter with the `curator_cli` binary and the `show_snapshots` action to keep tabs on snapshots from the command-line.  I do not recall offhand whether the snapshot start date was included in the verbose output, but I do not think I did.  That could be added, potentially.
While this is a nice-to-have we can always write your own Python script that uses the Elasticsearch and Curator APIs to do what you want...

Our specific use case is that we need to allow users to specify argument values that begin with a hyphen. The only way to accomplish this is to use the equal version of argument specification: 
--in=--some-user-value

We want the help messages to guide users to this--can get some of the functionality using metavar, but to force the equal to appear, we have to override the help parser. 

Current help message without overriding HelpFormatter (notice no '=' after the first '--in')
usage: az ml service create batch [-h] [--output {json,tsv,table,jsonc}]
                                  [--in <input_name>:[<default_value>]
                                  [--in=...]]

Our desired help message is (notice the '=' after first '--in'):
usage: az ml service create batch [-h] [--output {json,tsv,table,jsonc}]
                                  [--in=<input_name>:[<default_value>]
                                  [--in=...]]

newoption
    {
trigger     = "glfw_include_dir",
value       = default_glfw_include_dir,
description = "GLFW 3.x include directory"
    }

newoption
    {
                        trigger     = "glfw_lib_name",
                        value       = default_glfw_lib_name,
                        description = "GLFW 3.x library name (glfw, glfw3)"
    }

    newoption
    {
trigger     = "glfw_lib_dir",
value       = default_glfw_lib_dir,
description = "(optional) GLFW 3.x library directory "
    }

    newoption
    {
trigger     = "enable_glfw",
value       = false,
description = "(optional) use GLFW 3.x library"
    }

if _OPTIONS["enable_glfw"] then

> Surely this is not a hugely complicated thing?

Any change affecting the metadata format isn't in the bag labeled "not hugely complicated things" ;-)

> If this is true (I'm just experimenting) then perhaps that could be enforced so that going forward it is documented that partial classes "perceive" their member ordering to be as described above?

I think what we're saying is that we're aren't seeing the value to be willing to make such a guarantee. Also, Roslyn is just one of our compilers. Mono has a separate C# compiler and there are many other compilers out there as well (such as F#). Even if we were to make the promise for Roslyn it wouldn't be a guarantee per ECMA335 spec (and adding additional constraints later is a breaking change from the existing spec). Of course, changes like this are doable in principle but I don't think we're seeing the value to justify such an impactful change.

On top of that, enforcing guarantees around token ordering might impose additional limitations we need to take into account as we're adding more constructs in the future. Assume, for instance, partial classes didn't exist and we made the guarantee you're asking for. Is adding partial classes now breaking the promise? I'd argue partial classes are much more useful than keeping the invariant you're asking for. That's another reason why I don't think we're willing to go down that path.

@JaniKarh Why not just write a convenience function for this, similar to [this one](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/tz/_common.py#L375)? I am not sure why you want to use `relativedelta` for this purpose, though - if you want an absolute difference in times (like a number of seconds), it seems like a `datetime.timedelta` would be the more appropriate data type for this purpose. `relativedelta` is used for situations where the absolute amount of time that passes (in seconds elapsed) between two dates changes depending on the date - e.g. "1 month" or "1 year".

It is indeed hard to avoid accidentally breaking things in any project of reasonable complexity, but, when you do discover you have broken things unintentionally, you really should roll back the change and shelve it for a major release, if at all possible. Knowing that a library tends to break things in minor releases does not do wonders for my confidence in relying on it. 

The speed difference is indeed very large (incidentally, I did not use strptime but wrote a dedicated 8601 parser in C).  You could perhaps add a note to the documentation of parse() saying that it should not be used for dates in known formats, and perhaps provide strptime expressions for a couple of common formats. That will help your users do the right thing, and also make changes to the parser that break things affect less people. I suspect that many people are lazy and just use dateutil to parse known format dates because there is no easy out of the box solution for the common formats (you have to hunt around to find dedicated modules for each format).

I haven't investigated this case specifically, but I have seen similar cases before when NCBI updates the alignment *for the same transcript and genome accessions and versions*. Discrepancies are usually on exon boundaries, and occur when the alignment is atrocious anyway. Presumably, the cause is subtle tuning of iterations of splign or perhaps the post-alignment selection process that NCBI uses. The details of that process are fluid and not public, AFAIK.

Look at the CIGAR you provided -- you don't want to use that alignment anyway!

Yes, mutable alignments like this mean that you can't trust projections in these regions.

Several years ago I proposed to NCBI and HGVS that we generate digests for the alignments themselves. That is, we treat alignments as first class entities so that (transcript, genome) accession pairs might actually have multiple alignments. (UTA uses a form of this idea already. If you find a transcript like NM_0123.4/0a419f, it means that the alignment changed and the hash becomes part of the name so that it's distinguishable from the current version.)

Both s3cmd and gsutil are capable of syncing between buckets server side, because both S3 and Google Cloud Storage have APIs to quickly copy files in the server. After working on a new project with marshmallow, I started using the `Method` field more heavily, and realized that it is a really powerful abstraction that could potentially bridge the gap here.

https://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api_reference.html#marshmallow.fields.Method

```py
from marshmallow import fields

import utils

class PetShopSchema(NamedSchema):
    pet = fields.Nested('PetSchema', only=('name', 'url'))
    foo = fields.Method(serialize='get_nested_fields', only=('foo', ('bar', ('baz', ))))

    def get_nested_fields(self, obj, field_names):
        return utils.get_nested_fields(obj, field_names)
```

It doesn't help at schema instantiation time, but it does give you the freedom to nest however you please.

Another option is to reproduce the pvl_singlediode.m Matlab API by switching our singlediode's output from a DataFrame a simple dictionary. The dictionary values would be scalars or arrays as determined by the numpy broadcasting rules.

Here's another try at the multiple function approach. We'd have the existing ``singlediode`` that works with either scalar or time series inputs, and these functions that handle IV curve stuff for scalar inputs. 

I don't know if one approach is better than the other.

```python
def singlediode_points_to_frame(pnt):
    """
    Converts a dict of scalars describing the 5 points of
    a single diode curve to a 5 row DataFrame with
    columns current and voltage.

    Useful for plotting.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    pnt: dict
        Typically the output of the singlediode function.
        Must have keys i_sc, p_mp, i_x, i_xx, v_oc.
        
    Returns
    -------
    fivepnts: DataFrame
        Columns labeled voltage, current.
        Rows labeled i_sc, p_mp, i_x, i_xx, v_oc.
    """
    fivepnts = {'i_sc': (pnt['i_sc'], 0),
                'p_mp': (pnt['i_mp'], pnt['v_mp']),
                'i_x': (pnt['i_x'], 0.5*pnt['v_oc']),
                'i_xx': (pnt['i_xx'], 0.5*(pnt['v_oc']+pnt['v_mp'])),
                'v_oc': (0, pnt['v_oc'])}
    fivepnts = pd.DataFrame(fivepnts, index=['current', 'voltage']).T
    fivepnts = fivepnts.sort_values(by='voltage')
    
    return fivepnts


def singlediode_ivcurve(module_parameters, photocurrent, saturation_current,
                        resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth, v_oc=None, num=50):
    """
    Creates a single diode IV curve with uniformly spaced points.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    See singlediode
    
    v_oc: None or float
        Will be calculated if None.
    num: int
        Number of IV curve samples.
        
    Returns
    -------
    ivcurve: DataFrame
        Columns labeled voltage, current.
        Rows labeled by point number.
    """
    
    if v_oc is None:
        v_oc = pvsystem.v_from_i(
            resistance_shunt, resistance_series, nNsVth, 0, saturation_current, photocurrent)
        
    voltage = np.linspace(0, v_oc, num)
    current = pvsystem.i_from_v(
        resistance_shunt, resistance_series, nNsVth, voltage, saturation_current, photocurrent)
    ivcurve = pd.DataFrame(np.array([voltage, current]).T, columns=['voltage', 'current'])
    
    return ivcurve


def singlediode_points_ivcurve(module_parameters, photocurrent, saturation_current,
                               resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth,
                               num=50):
    """
    Solves the single diode model for scalar inputs and returns an IV curve
    and the 5 IV curve characterization points.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    see singlediode
    
    Returns
    -------
    fivepnts: DataFrame
        Columns labeled voltage, current.
        Rows labeled i_sc, p_mp, i_x, i_xx, v_oc.

    ivcurve: DataFrame
        Columns labeled voltage, current.
        Rows labeled by point number.
    """
    
    fivepnts = pvsystem.singlediode(
        module_parameters, photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth)
    fivepnts = singlediode_points_to_frame(fivepnts)

    ivcurve = singlediode_ivcurve(
        module_parameters, photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth,
        v_oc=fivepnts.ix['v_oc', 'voltage'])
    
    return fivepnts, ivcurve


resistance_shunt = 16
resistance_series = 0.094
nNsVth = 0.473
saturation_current = 1.943e-09
photocurrent = 7

fivepnts, ivcurve = singlediode_points_ivcurve(
    module_parameters, photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(ivcurve['voltage'], ivcurve['current'])
ax.scatter(fivepnts['voltage'], fivepnts['current'], c='k', s=36, zorder=10)
ax.set_xlim(0, None)
ax.set_ylim(0, None)
ax.set_ylabel('current (A)')
ax.set_xlabel('voltage (V)')
# as shown above
```

I have been coming back to this issue from time to time. I think the issue stems from the `sacred` process capturing all 1 and 2 FDs. For example:

1. The sacred experiment logs to FD 1.
2. Sacred redirects the FD 1 process and redirects it to the `tee` process's FD 1.
3. The `tee` process outputs the message to temp file and the tee's process FD 1.
4. Since the `tee` process  is a child process of the `sacred` process, `sacred` will redirect this to `tee`.
5. The cycle continues. 

This may block `sacred` from getting a chance to read the temp file.

I have looked into how other tools handle this problem. It seems like they all use multiple threads/processes and have queues to communicate between them.

@lurch, @willmcgugan: if we decide to go for NewerFilesWalker implementation then how do we cope with the single file copy method? 

```
def copy_file(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path):
…
```

This method (obviously) doesn't get a walker as input parameter, so how do we handle the case copy_if_newer?

Thinking more generally, what should be the responsibility of a Walker object? IMHO it should descend (aka walk) *one* file system tree. It shouldn’t access external file systems objects to perform some kind of chekcs (e.g. check the file modification time). 

IMHO, the proper position for the copy_if_newer flag is in the functions signatures inside the copy module (copy_file, copy_fs, etc). Those functions get 2 file systems in input, and they are properly positioned to execute actions on both those file systems (e.g. compare the modification time of a file).

So what is the solution?

A simple solution could be, use the (ugly) python features *args: copy_file will become: 

`def copy_file(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path, *args):`

where args can handle any parameter (e.g. copy_if_newer). IMHO this Python feature make code really unreadable, but in some scenario I have to admit is extremely powerful.

If we want to consider a more disruptive refactoring ;-) and if we go back to the old good times of OOP, some OOP guru (may be was Grady Booch?) was used to say: “if your problem gets more complex add a class!” So, let’s add a class. We can refactor the functions of the copy module in a copy class. Something like:

```
class copy(object):
	def __init__(self, src_fs, dst_fs, copy_if_newer=False, walker=None):
		self._src_fs = src_fs
		self._dst_fs = dst_fs
		self._walker = walker
		self._copy_if_newer=copy_if_newer

	def set_copy_if_newer(self, value):
		self._copy_if_newer = value

	def set_walker(self, walker):
		self._walker = walker

	def copy_file(self,src_path, dst_fs, dst_path):
		pass

	def copy_structure():
		pass

```
In this case we don’t have the problem of too many parameters for the copy_x functions anymore. 
Moreover a class here makes a lot of sense, because the Copy object could be reused multiple time, in the same snip of code. Immagine something like that:

```
backup= Copy("osfs:///data/", "osfs:///storage/")
backup.copy_file("/logs/error.log", "/log/error.log")
backup.set_copy_if_newer(True)
backup.copy_file("/logs/access.log", "/access/error.log")
...
```

Moreover, we can immagie that in future the Copy class can growth to achieve rsync like functionalities. For instance we might want to implement a very useful mirroring (between filesystems) function. Or we might want to develop a copy_dir based on regex, where dst_path is calculated from src_path using a regex, thus implementing a path transformation (see below, the example of backup copy refactored in one line, code not tested!):

```
backup= Copy("osfs:///data/", "osfs:///storage/")
backup.copy_dir_with_transform("/", "\/.+\/(?P<type>.+)\.log, "/{type}/{type}.log")
```

I understand that this second scenario (refactor to a class) will require a lot of effort in refactoring tests and also documentation. 
Please consider this only a Sunday morning rant ;-)

What do you think guys?

Ciao

We've certainly been figuring out how to support this and even have code for detecting conda environments that have `ipykernel` installed as a python package.

This comes down to two choices:

* Wait for us to finish off https://github.com/nteract/nteract/issues/357 and https://github.com/nteract/nteract/pull/1264 as well as ship it
* Run the [`ipykernel install` command inside your conda env](http://ipython.readthedocs.io/en/stable/install/kernel_install.html#installing-the-ipython-kernel):

```
source activate myenv
python -m ipykernel install --user --name myenv --display-name "Python (myenv)"
```

Thanks for your patience as we get it all situated.

Should be possible I think, you will probably have to edit kernelspecs yourself.
When nteract loads it's looking for kernelspec files which define kernel details (name, location, etc).

When you load nteract on Windows I think nteract is checking the Windows location but your kernelspecs are installed on the Linux subsystem.

The Windows user location is `%APPDATA%\jupyter\kernels` which on mine is `C:\Users\<name>\AppData\Roaming\jupyter\kernels`

The Linux user location for kernelspecs is `~/.local/share/jupyter/kernels`

You can try and copy your kernelspecs from Linux location to the Windows location and then try to launch nteract again. Windows needs to be able to access and launch Python from the linux subsystem file location. Check out the kernel.json file to make sure Windows can interpret the file location.

https://jupyter-client.readthedocs.io/en/latest/kernels.html#kernelspecs

    import simuvex
    class __libc_init(simuvex.SimProcedure):
        ADDS_EXITS = True
        NO_RET = True
        local_vars = ('main', 'argc', 'argv', 'envp')

        def run(self, raw_args, unused, slingshot, structors):
            offset = self.state.arch.bits / 8 
            readlen = self.state.arch.bits / 8 
            endness = self.state.arch.memory_endness
            self.main = slingshot;
            self.argc = self.state.memory.load(raw_args + 0 * offset, readlen, endness=endness)
            argc_val = self.state.se.any_int(self.argc)
            self.argv = self.state.memory.load(raw_args + 1 * offset, readlen, endness=endness)
           self.envp= self.state.memory.load(raw_args + (1 + argc_val + 1) * offset, readlen, endness=endness)
            self.call(self.main, (self.argc, self.argv, self.envp), 'after_slingshot')

Like explained above, the new SLAAC address for OTBR is added at wpantund when new prefixes is found. https://github.com/openthread/wpantund/blob/master/src/wpantund/NCPInstanceBase-Addresses.cpp#L767 Compared with CLI version, I guess the phenomenon of the two consecutive Child Update Request for OTBR may be caused by the slight time consumption due to additional spinel transactions, and when adding the new address, the Child Update Request caused by network data update has already been ready or sent. I thought triggerring an MLE Child Update Request on OT_CHANGED_THREAD_NETDATA might be intended for SED as previously we used unicast Data Response to send new network data to SED. It should be no issue as now we are using unicast Child Update Request instead and there would be Child Update Response to notify the parent of the network data version the child has. Not sure if it is necessary for MED, as it has more chance to learn if there is newer network data (e.g. through the periodic MLE advertisement from the parent which has higher network data version in Leader Data TLV). However I agree that this PR might not be a proper solution as the other stack vendors may still use unicast Data Response to send new network data to SED. @shuuchen Can we close this issue?

> No, because the module initialization code (inside the PYBIND11_MODULE body) never gets executed. 

I'm confused. How would the module initialization get executed in a C++ test driver? By calling `module::import()`?

As far as organization, moving it to another header is a good idea.  It can't *quite* be all moved, though: the `class_::init(py::init_factory &&)` member function is still needed (because it needs to pass the `class_<...>` type to `execute()`).

The reason is simple: Python 2.7 ``pickle`` calls the pybind11 internal initializers in an incorrect order, which leads to a crash, while ``cPickle`` doesn't. For Python 3, there is only ``pickle``, so everything works by default.

I won't have time to chase down why Python 2.7 ``pickle`` does it this way, and if there is a way to avoid the segfault (as Python 2.7 is deprecated in any case). Patches are welcomed though (it sounds like this is important to you).

The thing is that docstrings are only for python-exposed functions, and are not meant to serve as a documentation of the underlying C++.

The functions exposed to Python could return anything depending on the input parameters and documenting overloads or return types does not really make sense for sphinx.

I don't think that we should try shoving a C++ mindset into the python-side documentation by adding return types.

My first thought is this particular distribution has a special case where if conf.d/ssl.conf (or whatever the default mod_ssl config file is called) is created as an empty file if it doesn't exist (touch it for example) before installing mod_ssl. I can see only one issue - that mod_ssl must be enabled for things to work - I am sure something could be worked out here though.

I don't think Gurobi's `update` is comparable the the `reset` that Felipe is mentioning. In my understanding, Gurobi uses `update` to actually "flush" a queue of changes (add variable etc.) so that later changes to the model (add constraint etc.) can be done consistently.

In our case, the `reset` would have to be called *before* any changes may be done, not *after* the changes (to make them have an effect). Also, it's only needed after a call of `optimize`.

Having the user call `reset` between solves is easier for PySCIPOpt, because it means we don't have to store copies of results, such as status, bounds or solutions, which would become invalid with `freeTransform`. It also means that we should catch errors because of the missing `freeTransform` and provide a nice error message.

Still a bit confused. You don't have to use pywebpush's VAPID. You can just pass in the header via the same thing I'm doing in the convenience function.
```
result = WebPusher(subscription_info).send(
        data,
        vapid_headers,
        content_encoding=content_encoding
)
```
Heck, you could cache the `WebPush()` object if you wanted and then just call the `.send()` method. 
Or call `.encode()` if you want to batch up your deliveries for later. 

The `vapid_headers` are just a dict of the `Authorization` header and the `Crypto-Key` (if you're using the older VAPID 'webpush' format.)

I can kinda see where pulling the aud makes sense for VAPID, but considering that VAPID is supposed to be "voluntary", I'm not sure that it's really pywebpush's problem. (Yes, I know that Google makes VAPID a good deal less Voluntary than the spec writers would prefer.)

For group A, modifying Arrow makes a lot more sense to me, since Plasma is part of Arrow, it makes sense to do all Plasma development there. Otherwise updates to Plasma will break the async client, so it's important to have the client tested in the Arrow CI.

For group B, I think it makes sense to do as much as possible in C++. This will make it easier to wrap from other languages like Java. It'd be ok to do something quick in Python using the existing C++ API, but that doesn't feel like a long-term solution.

For group C, creating an event loop for every blocking call sounds very heavyweight to me, but I could be completely wrong about that, so it's a question of performance. However, if we want to use an underlying async implementation, then that would require the async implementation to be in C++.

`django-helpdesk` on its own does not do any type of scanning. For spam, you can optionally enable `akismet` support for detecting spam, check the documentation for more details. I don't personally use it, so if you give it a try and find any problems let us know. That said, I believe `akismet` will only scan the text of comments. To my knowledge it still won't scan the actual attachments.

One option is to have your ticket mail sent to a Gmail account so Google does the spam/virus checking for you, and then setup `django-helpdesk` to use IMAP to pull email from the Gmail account.

Apologies -- maybe I should be a bit more precise and say begin `_rate=1.0`. My program is essentially an execution environment for smaller programs ("apps"). It houses an internal clock, an event bus of sorts, and a bunch of registered user apps (python classes that do their own thing and utilize the automation api). I expose many methods that allow my users to schedule callbacks (sync or async!) to run under various contexts: at a specific time, after a given duration, on a matching incoming event, etc. Naturally, it would be pretty painful for a user to want to test their little application if the have a callback which only runs "every Saturday", or at a specific time far away in the future.

As-is in your implementation with `self._rate=1.0`, the virtual base time value will begin at the real base time value and then move forward in time based on the same sentiment you initially started with in `MockClock`. If you want to move at 2x the speed of time, set `MockClock(rate=2.0)`, or if you want to move at a third the speed of time, set `MockClock(rate=0.3)` and so on. **The key here being that we *begin* at the current time, instead of Unix epoch.**

In my variant, I've also allowed my user to define a "start" to their clock, thus pulling back or pushing forward the virtual base attribute.

In your case, it might not make any sense at all for `MockClock` to keep the rate operative, however it is definitely a mandatory feature in my implementation of a Clock.

It's not pytester. There's some unawaited coroutine somewhere (a routine
called 'fix1', apparently), which causes a warning when it gets garbage
collected. That warning gets issued in a weird context (the coroutine
__del__ method). We're using '-W error' to convert warnings into errors.
And it turns out that 3.5 had a bug where the __del__ method forgot to
account for the possibility that the warning it's trying to issue could
turn into an error, and it segfaults. Eventually someone noticed, but not
until after 3.5.2 was out :-).

Probably the solution is to make sure the coroutine is awaited? Unawaited
coroutines are basically always a bug. It's possible it's needed here b/c
you're intentionally testing a case where things are broken, in which case
there are some other options (trio has some tests like that too).

I see. Perhaps you could help me work around this issue with a project I'm working on.

In the project I have terminals similar to the following for unquoted strings, decimal numbers, and hexadecimal numbers:
```
root: STR | DEC | HEX

STR: /\S/+
DEC: ("0".."9")+
HEX: ("0x" ("0".."9" | "A".."F" | "a".."f")+)
```

I'd like to be able to have things like "10" always parse as a decimal and "0xFF" as a hexadecimal, but the non-deterministic behavior is causing them to sometimes be interpreted as strings.

Is there an easy way I can refactor this to work around the unexpected behavior?

The primary use case that I have had for conditional lexing is parsing byte strings. By entering an exclusive lexing state, I can use more simple regexes to parse out the byte string. I can also catch errors that would otherwise be accepted with a large regex.

**Example:** the byte jump section in the original byte string [regex](https://github.com/plyara/plyara/blob/master/plyara.py#L711) will accept any number range for the jump; this includes cases where the first number value is smaller than the second (which is not valid). 

A simpler regex can be used with an exclusive lexing state to capture the two numbers in the jump range and subsequently compared for validity, as seen [here](https://github.com/PUNCH-Cyber/YaraGuardian/blob/master/plyara/plyara/plyara.py#L721-L728).
